summary: Venetian Mannerist painter (Jacopo Robusti, c. 1518–1594), nicknamed 'Il Furioso' or 'little dyer' (father was a silk dyer). Briefly a student of Titian. Best known for a diagonal Last Supper at San Giorgio Maggiore, The Miracle of the Slave (his 'first mature masterpiece'), Il Paradiso in the Doge's Palace (one of the largest paintings on canvas), and The Origin of the Milky Way. He aspired to combine 'Michelangelo's design and Titian's color.'

works.name: The Last Supper (1592-94)
works.description: Final work, at San Giorgio Maggiore in Venice. Famous for its diagonal table composition, ghostly translucent angels orbiting an oil lamp, and Christ's bright halo.

comprehensive_summary:
Tintoretto (Jacopo Robusti, c. 1518–1594) was a Venetian Mannerist painter nicknamed 'Il Furioso' for his impetuous painting style, and also called 'little dyer' (Tintoretto) because his father was a silk dyer. He was briefly a student of Titian but was reportedly thrown out of his studio after just three days. He famously placed the inscription 'Michelangelo's design and Titian's color' in his studio as an artistic ambition. Jean-Paul Sartre called him 'the first film director,' and Pierre-Jean Mariette noted a joke about his three pencils (gold, silver, and iron) reflecting his inconsistency. He beat out rivals including Veronese to decorate the Sala dell'Albergo at the Scuola di San Rocco, on the strength of his painting St. Roch in Glory.

His most frequently clued work is The Last Supper (1592-94) at San Giorgio Maggiore, famous for its diagonal table composition, lit by two sources: Christ's bright halo and a hanging oil lamp surrounded by ghostly translucent angels. A cat sniffs inside a basket in the foreground, and a woman reaches into a basket rather than watching the meal. It was his final work, with son Domenico helping with drapery. An earlier 1547 version was painted for the church of San Marcuola.

The Miracle of the Slave (1548), Tintoretto's 'first mature masterpiece,' was painted for the Scuola Grande di San Marco. It depicts Saint Mark swooping down from the sky (upside-down, in a red robe and orange cape) to save a nude man from execution; the man was to be blinded for traveling to Alexandria to venerate the saint. Broken tools (hammer, axe) litter the ground, a white-turbaned man stands above, and a knight of Provence sits on a throne at right. Originally intended as a ceiling painting, its corners were cut to make it octagonal. The background features a marble temple with four pilasters topped with human forms, and an observer from the East is perched on a balcony railing in the upper-left corner.

Il Paradiso (c. 1588-92) in the Doge's Palace (Sala del Maggior Consiglio) is often called the largest painting on canvas ever and the largest from the Renaissance, depicting over 500 figures on a 74-by-30-foot canvas. It replaced Guariento's Coronation of the Virgin fresco, which was destroyed in the 1577 fire. Michael and Gabriel flank Christ greeting a star-crowned Mary; son Domenico helped complete it. The commission was won after Veronese was unable to complete it.

The Origin of the Milky Way (c. 1575) shows ten gold stars shooting from Hera's breast as infant Hercules is pulled away by a levitating Zeus. Multiple cherubs appear: one with a bow in the lower right, one holding a chain supporting Hera's arm, and one with a net pursuing an eagle with thunderbolts. Two peacocks stand on a black cloud at lower right. Per Erna Mandowsky, a lower half depicting blooming lilies was cut from the painting; it may have been inspired by the Byzantine botanical textbook the Geoponica.

His St. Mark cycle includes Finding the Body of St. Mark (saint above his own pale corpse in an Alexandrian crypt). Other works include Miracle of the Bronze Serpent (ceiling painting for Scuola di San Rocco), Saint George and the Dragon (unusually centering on the corpse and fleeing princess rather than the knight), The Deliverance of Arsinoe (two nude women in a gondola, one kissing a knight, fleeing Julius Caesar), Bacchus and Ariadne with Venus (referencing Venice's wedding to the sea), a painting of Mars hiding under a table as Vulcan inspects Venus for signs of an affair, and Jacob's Ladder. His family workshop included daughter Marietta (Tintoretta, whose work was indistinguishable from his) and sons Marco and Domenico. Multiple 19th-century painters (Pagliano, Cogniet, Girardet) depicted Tintoretto painting his dead daughter. Thomas Bernhard's novel Old Masters features an 82-year-old critic who visits Tintoretto's White-Bearded Man every other day.
